author: Augustine of Hippo
book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.

Augustin Deceived by Familiarity with the Manichæans, and by the Succession of Victories Over Ignorant Christians Reported by Them. The Manichæans are Likewise Easily Refuted from the Knowledge of Sin and the Will.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: Augustin Deceived by Familiarity with the Manichæans, and by the Succession of Victories Over Ignorant Christians Reported by Them. The Manichæans are Likewise Easily Refuted from the Knowledge of Sin and the Will.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But two things especially, which easily lay hold upon that unwary age, urged me through wonderful circuits.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: One of these was familiarity, suddenly, by a certain false semblance of goodness, wrapped many times around my neck as a certain sinuous chain.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: The other was, that I was almost always noxiously victorious in arguing with ignorant Christians who yet eagerly attempted, each as he could, to defend their faith.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: By which frequent success the ardor of youth was kindled, and by its own impulse rashly verged upon the great evil of stubbornness.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For this kind of wrangling, after I had become an auditor among them, whatever I was able to do either by my own genius, such as it was, or by reading the works of others, I most gladly devoted to them alone.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Accordingly from their speeches ardor in disputations was daily increased, from success in disputations love for them [the Manichæans].

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Whence it resulted that whatever they said, as if affected by certain strange disorders, I approved of as true, not because I knew it to be true, but because I wished it to be.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: So it came about that, however slowly and cautiously, yet for a long time I followed men that preferred a sleek straw to a living soul.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: So be it, I was not able at that time to distinguish and discern sensible from intelligible things, carnal forsooth from spiritual.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: It did not belong to age, nor to discipline, nor even to any habit, nor, finally, to any deserts; for it is a matter of no small joy and felicitation: had I not thus been able at length even to grasp that which in the judgment of all men nature itself by the laws of the most High God has established?
